title: Sets
description: Master C# HashSet<T> for unique element storage, set operations, and deduplication in coding interviews.

sections.title: HashSet Basics: Add, Contains, Remove
sections.explanation: `HashSet<T>` stores unique elements with O(1) average Add, Contains, and Remove. `Add()` returns false if element already exists. `Contains()` checks membership. `Remove()` returns false if element wasn't present. Use `.Count` for size. Initialize with collection: `new HashSet<int>(array)`. HashSet does not maintain insertion order.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashSet<string> and add "apple", "banana", "cherry", "apple" (duplicate). Print the count, whether it contains "banana", remove "banana", print the count again and whether it still contains "banana".

validation.message: Count 3 (no duplicate), contains banana True, after remove count 2 and contains False.
Use HashSet<string> with Add, Contains, and Remove.

exercise.hint: Add returns True/False but we're printing Count and Contains results here.

sections.title: Set Operations: IntersectWith, UnionWith, ExceptWith
sections.explanation: HashSet provides in-place set operations: `IntersectWith()` keeps only common elements, `UnionWith()` adds all elements from another collection, `ExceptWith()` removes elements found in another collection, `SymmetricExceptWith()` keeps elements in either set but not both. These mutate the set in-place. For non-mutating checks, use `Overlaps()`, `IsSubsetOf()`, `IsSupersetOf()`.

exercise.prompt: Given sets A = {1, 2, 3, 4} and B = {3, 4, 5, 6}. Compute and print the intersection, union, and elements in A but not in B (difference). Print each as a sorted comma-separated list. Use sorted output via a temporary sorted list.

validation.message: Use IntersectWith, UnionWith, and ExceptWith for set operations.

exercise.hint: Copy a into new HashSet before each operation to avoid mutation. Then call IntersectWith(b), etc.

sections.title: Deduplication with HashSet
sections.explanation: The most common interview use of HashSet: removing duplicates from a collection. Pass any IEnumerable to the HashSet constructor: `new HashSet<int>(array)`. Convert back with `ToArray()` or `ToList()`. This preserves unique elements but not order. To preserve order, iterate manually and add to a HashSet, only keeping first occurrences.

exercise.prompt: Given string[] words = {"hello", "world", "hello", "foo", "world", "bar", "foo"}, remove duplicates while preserving the first-occurrence order. Print the deduplicated words joined by spaces. Also print the count of unique words.

validation.message: Use HashSet<string> with Add() to track seen elements.

exercise.hint: Use seen.Add(word)—it returns true only for new elements. When true, add to result list.

sections.title: HashSet Pattern: Complement Lookup
sections.explanation: A powerful interview pattern: use a HashSet to find complements. For two-sum with unique answers, iterate and check if `target - current` is in the set. For finding pairs, duplicates, or matching elements, HashSet gives O(1) lookups vs O(n) linear scan. This turns O(n²) brute force into O(n).

exercise.prompt: Write a method `bool HasPairWithSum(int[] nums, int target)` that returns true if any two distinct elements sum to target, using a HashSet for O(n) time. Test with {1, 4, 6, 3, 8} and targets 7, 15, and 20. Print each boolean.

validation.message: Use HashSet to store seen numbers and check for complement.

exercise.hint: For each n, check if seen.Contains(target - n). If yes, return true. Otherwise Add(n).
